children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 157 – Financing of the United Nations Mission in South Sudan – Budget performance for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Mission in South Sudan – Report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

table_content.text: Proposal submitted by the Secretary-General for 2026/27
Adjustment recommended by the Advisory Committee for 2026/27
Recommendation of the Advisory Committee for 2026/27

a	Estimates as at 28 February 2026.
	b	Excludes one-time requirements for termination entitlements payable to staff members.

a	Estimates as at 28 February 2026.
	b	Excludes one-time requirements for termination entitlements payable to staff members.

children.text: 1.	During its consideration of the advance version of the report on the financing of the United Nations Mission in South Sudan (UNMISS) (A/80/621), the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions met with representatives of the Secretary-General, who provided additional information and clarification, concluding with written responses received on 19 March 2026. The comments and recommendations of the Committee on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations are contained in its report (A/80/604), and those pertaining to the findings and recommendations of the Board of Auditors on the United Nations peacekeeping operations for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 can be found in its related report (A/80/633).

text: II.	Budget performance report for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025

children.text: 2.	By its resolution 78/306, the General Assembly appropriated an amount of $1,237,887,300 gross ($1,206,006,100 net) for the maintenance of the Mission for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025. A total allotment was issued for the Mission in the amount of $1,213,368,500 (or 98.0 per cent of the appropriation). Expenditure for the period totalled $1,199,382,400 gross ($1,167,408,700 net), with a budget implementation rate of 96.9 per cent, which reflects: (a) higher-than-budgeted expenditure for military and police personnel ($7,794,900, or 1.4 per cent), attributable primarily to expenditure deferred from the 2022/23 period and charged to the 2024/25 period; (b) lower-than-budgeted expenditure for civilian personnel ($12,377,200, or 3.5 per cent) attributable to higher actual average vacancy rates; and (c) lower-than-budgeted expenditure for operational costs ($33,922,600, or 10.0 per cent), attributable primarily to the non-deployment of additional military and police personnel for which provisions for operational costs were included in the approved budget, lower costs for the rental and operations of fixed-wing aircraft and the prioritization of resources aimed at curtailing operational costs in response to the ongoing liquidity challenges.

3.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that redeployments in the amount of $7,794,900 to group I (military and police personnel) from group II (civilian personnel) and group III (operational costs) resulted from expenditures deferred from the 2022/23 period and charged to the 2024/25 period as part of the Mission’s reprioritization to cover funding shortfalls. The Advisory Committee trusts that more detailed information on redeployments will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, in particular on activities curtailed and other lower-than-budgeted expenditures that allowed for resources to be redeployed and the criteria used by Missions to reprioritize resources.

4.	The Advisory Committee notes from the information provided to it that, as at 30 June 2025, unliquidated obligations for 2024/25 amounted to $138,502,100 (or 11.2 per cent of the appropriation), representing an increase of $3,204,500 compared with the amount of $135,297,600 as at 30 June 2024 for the 2023/24 period. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that, as at 31 January 2026, unliquidated obligations had decreased to $12,354,000 (or 1.0 per cent of the total appropriation) for the 2024/25 period, comprising military and police personnel ($4,067,000), civilian personnel ($92,800) and operational costs ($8,194,200).

5.	The unencumbered balance, in gross terms, amounts to $38,504,900, or 3.1 per cent of the overall level of resources approved for the financial period. A detailed analysis of variances is provided in section III of the report of the Secretary-General. Information provided to the Advisory Committee indicates that other revenue and adjustments for the 2024/25 period amount to $12,319,800 comprising: (a) investment revenue ($5,510,400); (b) other/miscellaneous revenue ($195,400); and (c) the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($6,614,000). The Committee notes that the Secretary-General, in paragraphs 82 and 130 of his report, proposes that credits arising from the above-mentioned revenue and adjustments be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates (see para. 55 below).
Matters pertaining to the report of the Board of Auditors

6.	In considering the report of the Secretary-General on the financing of UNMISS, the Advisory Committee also had before it the report of the Board of Auditors on the accounts of the United Nations peacekeeping operations for the financial period ended 30 June 2025 (A/80/5 (Vol. II)). Regarding UNMISS, the Board noted issues in terms of the belated recognition of cash losses, weapons and ammunition management and the need to more strategically mobilize financial and human resources regarding mandates related to disarmament, demobilization and reintegration processes. The Advisory Committee trusts that the recommendations will be implemented in a timely manner.

text: III.	Information on performance for the current period

children.text: 7.	Regarding current and projected expenditure for the period from 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 28 February 2026, expenditure amounted to $629,196,900, or 53.0 per cent of the appropriation of $1,186,358,500. At the end of the current period, the estimated total expenditure would amount to $1,008,404,700, with a projected underexpenditure of $177,953,800, or 15 per cent, against the approved resources.

8.	The report of the Secretary-General provides information on the Mission’s revised operational posture since October 2025 in response to prevailing liquidity constraints and its impact on mandate implementation (A/80/621, paras. 48–50). The Advisory Committee was informed, upon enquiry, that UNMISS has been operating under persistent liquidity constraints for the past 30 months and that short-term planning has caused persistent operational and mandate delivery constraints, with a significant impact on the Mission’s ability to effectively implement its mandate. The Mission has implemented austerity measures to address the liquidity situation by means of a hub-based support model to maintain mandate delivery and a structured approach focused on cash preservation, operational continuity, the preservation of vendor confidence and strengthened financial discipline. The temporary mitigating measures have preserved compliance and continuity, thereby alleviating the disruption of services and operations and the resulting adverse impacts on mandate delivery. The extended recruitment freeze has also resulted in significantly reduced capacity, including critical capacity.

9.	The Advisory Committee was informed that the implementation of the contingency plan includes the repatriation of uniformed personnel, a reduction of civilian personnel and reduced operational expenditures. The Mission’s constrained operational capacity has resulted in reinforced collaboration with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes and non-governmental organizations, and in a revision of its geographical coverage, as indicated in the table below, whereby two field offices and two operating bases have been closed, six operating bases are in the closure phase and six field offices are to be retained. Civilian staff were also reduced by 15 per cent at mission headquarters and in the remaining field offices, in conjunction with the downsizing of two field offices.
Table 1 – Evolution of the Mission’s geographical presence and operational capacity

table_content.text: Juba, Malakal, Bentiu, Wau, Rumbek, Aweil, Kuacjok, Torit, Yambio, Bor

Juba, Malakal, Bentiu, Wau, Yambio, Bor

Yei, Pibor, Akobo, Renk, Bunj, Kodok, Leer, Pariang

Pibor, Kodok, Renk

Closure ongoing: Akobo, Bunj, Leer, Pariang

children.text: 10.	The Advisory Committee notes the measures adopted by the Mission for implementing the contingency plan and that the adjustments in geographical presence and operational capacity have significantly constrained the ability of the Mission to carry out its core mandated functions. The Committee also notes that UNMISS applied a prioritization approach based on protecting minimum operating requirements and critical mandate activities considered to be non‑discretionary with a hub-based support model to maintain mandate delivery. The Committee trusts that all efforts will be undertaken to ensure the security and safety of personnel. The Committee also trusts that an update on and more detailed assessment of the impact of the contingency plan on the implementation of the Mission’s mandate will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the report and in the next performance report.

11.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 10 February 2026, a total of $16,677,991,900 had been assessed on Member States in respect of UNMISS since its inception. Payments received as at the same date amounted to $16,056,573,900, leaving an outstanding balance of $621,418,000. As at 2 March 2026, the cash available amounted to $140,849,830, resulting from borrowing by active peacekeeping missions, and was not sufficient to cover the three-month operating reserve of $198,846,400 (excluding reimbursements to troop- and police-contributing countries). The Advisory Committee notes that the outstanding contributions of $621,418,000 represent approximately 52.4 per cent of the appropriation of $1,186,358,500 for the 2025/26 period. The Committee reiterates its concern regarding the serious and persistent challenge of the cash position of UNMISS, and the continued need for borrowing of funds by UNMISS to meet its operational requirements. The Committee recalls that the General Assembly has repeatedly urged all Member States to fulfil their financial obligations, as set out in the Charter of the United Nations, on time, in full and without conditions (see resolution 79/310, para. 2; see also A/79/724/Add.12, para. 9).

12.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 31 December 2025, the outstanding balance for troop costs and contingent-owned equipment amounted to $56,880,100 and $152,976,900, respectively. The Committee was also informed that, as at 5 February 2026, $3,939,000 had been paid to settle 155 death and disability claims since the inception of the Mission, and five claims were pending. The Advisory Committee trusts that all outstanding liabilities and claims will be settled in a timely manner.

text: IV.	Proposed budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027

children.text: A.	Mandate and planning assumptions

13.	The mandate of UNMISS was established by the Security Council in its resolution 1996 (2011) and was most recently extended until 30 April 2026 in resolution 2779 (2025). The planning assumptions and mission support initiatives for the 2026/27 period are summarized in section I.C of the report of the Secretary-General (A/80/621).

14.	The report of the Secretary-General indicates that the security situation in South Sudan continues to be volatile, notwithstanding some progress made in the political process, and it is expected to worsen in the period leading up to the elections (ibid., para. 73). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed of the volatile security and political landscape in South Sudan and that clarity is pending on the type, number and level of elections that could realistically be held by December 2026 and on any proposed amendments to the Revitalized Agreement on the Resolution of the Conflict in the Republic of South Sudan. The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the operational environment of the Mission, and its implication on budgetary requirements, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in future budget reports.
Impact of the UN80 Initiative and culture of efficiency

15.	Information on efficiency gains is provided in section II.C of the report of the Secretary-General. The cost estimates for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 take into account efficiency initiatives under military contingent personnel, civilian personnel and operational costs in the amount of $24,637,700. Estimated efficiencies for 2026/27 represent a 2.1 per cent reduction compared to the approved budget for 2025/26, in the context of an overall 10.9 per cent proposed reduction in the budget (see para. 20 below). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the implementation of the contingency plan through the recalibration of resources and Mission presence to continue effective mandate implementation, including by revisiting the modalities and frequency of operations, focusing efforts on high-risk areas, protection-of-civilians activities and early warning and targeted engagements, required a determination of permanent reductions as efficiency gains, the implementation of the civilian staffing review recommendations and organizational optimization and streamlining in the spirit of the UN80 Initiative. The Committee was also informed that the reduced Mission presence will affect the already limited but essential force protection support available to humanitarian actors operating in specific high-risk areas, notably along the Torit-Kapoeta corridor in Eastern Equatoria, the Bor-Pibor route in Jonglei and the Abiemnom-Mayom axis in Unity.

16.	As regards the reversibility of the contingency plan, the Advisory Committee was informed that, given the scope of the required cost reductions within the current financial year, more sites have been closed and the field offices are considered permanently closed. Although the reduction in the Mission’s footprint has implications on mandate implementation and the Mission no longer has the capacity to sustain all the interventions as planned or to respond to multiple crisis situations simultaneously, UNMISS continues to prioritize and adapt its operations while still implementing all four pillars of its mandate, including supporting humanitarian work within its remaining capacity and reach. The Advisory Committee notes that the efficiencies presented by UNMISS represent a small percentage of proposed reductions in the 2026/27 budget. While some proposed reductions reflect structural efficiency gains resulting from process improvements, a significant portion is primarily driven by liquidity constraints and temporary expenditure compression. The Committee trusts that further information on the connection between the proposed reductions and changes in the expected accomplishments and outputs proposed for the Mission for 2026/27 will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, to allow for a better assessment of the sustainability and long–term implications of such measures on the implementation of the Mission’s mandate. The Committee will further discuss efficiency measures in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Electoral support

17.	The Security Council, in its resolution 2779 (2025), mandated UNMISS to use its good offices and provide appropriate technical assistance and advice to the Revitalized Transitional Government of National Unity and relevant stakeholders, based on realistic planning assumptions and clear timelines. UNMISS will continue to advance its strategic priorities as outlined in the multi-year strategic vision (2024) to support the implementation of the Revitalized Agreement. Support will be extended to key government institutions to facilitate the conduct of credible, peaceful and inclusive elections (A/80/621, summary and para. 70).

18.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed of the status of the benchmarks and indicators of achievement for phase one electoral support (as at 20 February 2026) and the expected sequencing of the benchmarks, and that the prospect of holding elections in December 2026 is increasingly unlikely within the established time frame due to the lack of tangible actions, including political decisions, legal clarity and funding. Nevertheless, continued support for the electoral process and particularly phase one, which focuses on creating an enabling environment for inclusive and peaceful elections, remains essential and highly relevant, and is in accordance with the current mandate. UNMISS electoral assistance includes support focused on the following four critical areas to prepare the foundations for an enabling environment conducive to elections: (a) fostering inclusive participation; (b) institutional strengthening and capacity-building for professional, transparent and accountable election management; (c) the establishment of an enabling legal framework for inclusive and credible elections; and (d) an enhanced environment for maintaining security and mitigating violence related to elections.

19.	The Advisory Committee was informed of the achievements and support provided by the Mission to date in terms of the legal framework, the delivery of over 167 capacity-building and training initiatives, as well as civic education and institution-building initiatives, election security coordination and support for international community coordination (see also paras. 22–24 below). The Committee was also informed that electoral support is delivered through an integrated framework led by UNMISS, based on its Security Council mandate and operationally complemented by the United Nations Development Programme. This arrangement reflects the recommendations of the 2021 electoral needs assessment mission and is operationalized through the integrated electoral assistance team. The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the achievability of holding elections in December 2026 will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
B.	Resource requirements

20.	Resource requirements of $1,057,591,600 for the period 2026/27 represent a decrease of $128,766,900, or 10.9 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $1,186,358,500 for the 2025/26 period. Information on variances between the apportionment for 2025/26 and the proposed resources for 2026/27 is contained in section III of the proposed budget.
Financial resources
(Thousands of United States dollars)
21.	Estimated non-budgeted contributions for the 2026/27 period amount to $68,031,600. The Advisory Committee notes with appreciation the continued non-budgeted contributions to UNMISS.
Resources for electoral support

22.	The report of the Secretary-General indicates that the support provided by the Mission to the electoral process in South Sudan will continue to follow a phased approach, with the proposed resource requirements for the 2026/27 period focused on phase one activities. The proposed budget for the 2026/27 period, as indicated in table 2, includes $7,842,300 for electoral support, reflecting a decrease of $2,820,900 compared with the apportionment of $10,663,200 for 2025/26 due to reduced requirements for civilian personnel owing to the proposed abolishment of six positions and the application of higher vacancy rates, as well as reduced requirements for operational costs. The continuation of 61 positions, comprising 53 general temporary assistance positions and 8 international United Nations Volunteer positions, is proposed (A/80/621, paras. 37, 38 and 70–72 and table 4; and A/79/724/Add.12, paras. 21 and 22).
Table 2 – Resource requirements for electoral support, 2024/25 to 2026/27
(Thousands of United States dollars)

23.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed of the abolishment of six positions, comprising four Electoral Officer (2 P-4 and 2 international United Nations Volunteer) and two Programme Management Assistant (General Service), which were based in the locations closed as part of the contingency plan. The vacancy rates for electoral staff are as follows: 71.4 per cent for international staff; 25 per cent for National Professional Officers; 90.9 per cent for national General Service staff; and 30 per cent for international United Nations Volunteers. The Advisory Committee notes the expected expenditure of $5,181,300 in the current 2025/26 period against an appropriation of $8,016,200 for general temporary assistance. The Committee also notes the proposed reduction in staffing requirements for the Electoral Affairs Division for the 2026/27 period, including the abolishment of positions following the closure of locations under the contingency plan and the application of higher vacancy rates in the budget assumptions. In this regard, the Committee notes the very high actual vacancy rates for electoral staff, in particular international and national General Service staff, in the current period. Taking into account the above, as well as the complementarity with the support provided by the United Nations Development Programme (see para. 19 above) and the uncertainties surrounding the electoral process in South Sudan, the Committee recommends that UNMISS undertake a further critical review of its electoral staffing requirements and vacant posts to be presented in the next budget submission, with a view to ensuring that proposed resources are fully aligned with realistic operational needs.

24.	The Advisory Committee was also informed of staffing requirements to implement the mandated tasks for phase one and to provide adequate assistance at the subnational level across South Sudan. The Committee nevertheless notes that the support provided by the Mission to the electoral process remains constrained by liquidity challenges, recruitment restrictions and the implementation of the contingency plan, which have limited its presence to 6 of the 10 states of South Sudan. The Committee further notes the consistent pattern of underexpenditure in operational cost categories related to electoral support, including expenditures of $477,200 against $625,900 for consultants and consulting services, $81,100 against $285,900 for official travel and $681,600 against $1,240,200 for other supplies, services and equipment in the 2024/25 period, as well as similar trends in the current period as at 31 December 2025. The Advisory Committee emphasizes that electoral support requirements should be calibrated in line with the evolving context and realistic timelines for the electoral process. Taking into account the pattern of underexpenditure, the proposed adjustment to staffing and the evolving operational environment, the Committee recommends a 10 per cent reduction to the proposed amounts under each of the following budget lines: $49,300 under consultants and consulting services; $26,500 under official travel; and $122,400 under other supplies, services and equipment.
1.	Military and police personnel
a	Represents the highest level of authorized strength.

25.	The proposed resources for military and police personnel for the 2026/27 period amount to $437,715,000, reflecting a decrease of $85,781,600, or 16.4 per cent, compared with the apportionment for 2025/26. The reduced requirements are reflected mainly under military contingents ($75,502,100), followed by United Nations police ($5,392,500), formed police units ($3,298,300) and military observers ($1,588,700), with variances explained in the proposed budget (A/80/621, sect. III).
Delayed deployment factors
(Percentage of approved strength)

26.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that adjustments to the composition and number of uniformed personnel at UNMISS have been made under the contingency plan. The Mission has undertaken a rationalization of its footprint, resulting in the closure or conversion of key hubs (see paras. 8, 9 and 15 above). Adjustments in capacity and function at the remaining bases allow for the redistribution of personnel, assets and support requirements, while the reduction or closure of the bases has had a direct and measurable impact on the protection footprint, as indicated in table 3.
Table 3 – Patrol days for the 2024/25, 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods

table_content.text: Force protection, airfield and road patrols

children.text: 27.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, for group I (military and police personnel), the Mission reduced its force and repatriated 4,985 uniformed personnel, of whom 1,547 are not scheduled to return during the 2026/27 period. Approximately 3,000 items of contingent-owned major equipment were affected by the implementation of the contingency plan and have ceased operation. The affected equipment is to remain in UNMISS until either the date of repatriation or the date of reactivation for mission operations. No contingent-owned major equipment has been repatriated in the implementation of the contingency plan during the current 2025/26 period. The Committee notes that, following the return of military and police personnel, the Mission expects an average deployment of 200 military observers, 11,226 military contingent personnel, 603 United Nations police personnel and 723 formed police personnel for the 2026/27 period, which is reflected in the proposed delayed deployment factor.
28.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for military and police personnel.
2.	Civilian personnel

29.	The proposed resources for civilian personnel for 2026/27 amount to $329,348,000, reflecting a decrease of $20,532,900, or 5.9 per cent, compared with the apportionment for 2025/26, attributable primarily to lower requirements under: (a) international staff, due mainly to the proposed abolishment of 56 posts and the nationalization of 2 posts; (b) national Professional staff, attributable primarily to the proposed abolishment of 20 National Professional Officer posts, offset in part by the proposed nationalization of 3 international United Nations Volunteer positions; (c) national General Service staff, due mainly to the proposed abolishment of 121 national General Service posts, offset in part by the proposed nationalization of 2 international posts and 1 international United Nations Volunteer position; and (d) United Nations Volunteers, due mainly to the proposed abolishment of 55 positions and the nationalization of 4 others, and to the application of a higher vacancy rate of 24.6 per cent compared with the rate of 9.1 per cent applied in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period (A/80/621, sect. III).
Civilian staffing review

30.	UNMISS conducted a civilian staffing review in the 2025/26 period pursuant to General Assembly resolution 76/274. The report of the Secretary-General indicates that the review was strongly guided by the maximizing of efficiencies and that its recommendations highlighted opportunities to repurpose resources through anticipated vacancies, shared services, regionalization and other efficiency measures. Several recommendations have been included in the proposed budget for the 2026/27 period, and the remaining recommendations will be implemented in a phased manner based on the Mission’s priorities and operational imperatives (ibid., para. 51).
Recommendations on posts and positions

31.	The Secretary-General proposes a total of 2,658 civilian posts and positions for 2026/27, comprising 864 international staff and 1,328 national staff (154 National Professional Officers and 1,174 national General Service staff), 62 positions funded through general temporary assistance, 404 United Nations Volunteers and 88 government-provided personnel, reflecting a net decrease of 256 posts and positions compared with the budget approved for 2025/26. The Secretary-General proposes the following staffing changes in accordance with the civilian staffing review:

(a)	The abolishment of nine posts (1 D-1, 1 P-4, 5 P-3 and 2 Field Service) and eight international United Nations Volunteer positions, as follows: (i) one Principal Coordination Officer (D-1) in the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General; (ii) three Civil Affairs Officer (international United Nations Volunteer) in the Civil Affairs Section; (iii) seven Human Rights Officer (1 P-4, 2 P-3 and 4 international United Nations Volunteer) in the Human Rights Division; (iv) one Administrative Assistant (international United Nations Volunteer) in the Transport Section; and (v) two Security Officer (P-3), one Close Protection Officer ( P-3) and two Security Officer (Field Service) in the Safety and Security Section;

(b)	The conversion of the following six posts and positions: (i) three Civil Affairs Officer positions from international United Nations Volunteer to National Professional Officer posts in the Civil Affairs Division; (ii) one post of Administrative Assistant from Field Service to national General Service in the Rule of Law and Security Institutions Section; (iii) one position of Supply Chain Assistant from international United Nations Volunteer to a national General Service post as Movement Control Assistant in the Office of the Chief of Supply Chain Management Service; and (iv) one post of Human Resources Assistant from Field Service to national General Service in the Human Resources Management Section;

(c)	The reassignment of nine posts (1 P-5, 1 P-3 and 7 Field Service) to strengthen the Office of the Chief of Staff, the Conduct and Discipline Team, the Property and Acquisition Management Section, the Distribution (Movement Control) Section and the Engineering Section;

(d)	The reclassification of two posts of Security Coordination Officer in the Safety and Security Section from the P-3 to the P-4 level to reflect the higher level of responsibilities and align the supervisory level of the Field Security Coordination Office from other field offices;

(e)	The redeployment of 27 posts (1 P-4, 3 P-3, 4 Field Service, 18 national General Service and 1 National Professional Officer) and 6 United Nations Volunteer positions (ibid., paras. 54–67 and tables 2 and 3).

32.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, following the implementation of the contingency plan, the Mission reduced its staffing by 815 posts and positions during the 2025/26 period. Of these reductions, 239 civilian posts are proposed to be abolished in the 2026/27 period as other cost-reduction measures. This is in addition to 17 abolishments proposed in line with the recommendations of the civilian staffing review (see para. 31 (a) above). The posts and positions proposed to be abolished under cost-reduction measures were deployed in the duty stations that have been closed and are not expected to be reopened during the 2026/27 period. Furthermore, all posts and positions proposed for abolishment are currently unencumbered and, of the 20 government-provided personnel that were repatriated in 2025/26, 19 are not scheduled to return in 2026/27. The Committee was also informed that, while UNMISS prioritized its core protection of civilians-related functions in line with its mandate, the reduction in footprint and resources has already reduced the Mission’s reach and early warning capabilities, limiting rapid response and sustained presence in hotspots, constraining community engagement and diminishing human rights monitoring and the conduct of triangulation and verification activities that underpin credible reporting. The hiring freeze has also reduced the number of deployed civilian personnel to unsustainable levels and could significantly impair the Mission’s ability to implement its mandate.
Continuation of general temporary assistance positions
33.	It is proposed to retain the following general temporary assistance positions:
(a)	one position of Senior Programme Management Officer/Chief of Mine Action Programme (P-5), established on 1 July 2022 to direct the implementation of the mine action programme in UNMISS;

(b)	61 positions in the Electoral Affairs Division, comprising 53 general temporary assistance positions and 8 international United Nations Volunteer positions, to carry out activities mandated in Security Council resolution 2779 (2025) (see paras. 22–24 above);

(c)	Eight temporary positions of Security Officer (Field Service) in the Security and Safety Section, to carry out critically essential duties and enable the Mission to continue to ensure the security and safety of mission personnel and assets (A/80/621, paras. 69–73 and table 4).
Nationalization

34.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, in the 2021/22 to 2026/27 period, UNMISS has nationalized or proposed to nationalize eight posts and positions. UNMISS has consistently maintained a workforce composition of approximately 60 per cent national staff and 40 per cent international staff, and the national staffing levels have been preserved with an average ratio of 1.5 national staff to every international staff despite mandate adjustments, austerity measures and structural realignments. Nationalization will continue to be assessed over future budget periods, in alignment with the Mission’s strategic priorities, operational requirements and the Organization’s overall financial situation. The Committee was informed that the ratio of national staff to international staff is expected decrease from 1.04 in 2025/26 to 1.03 in 2026/27. The Advisory Committee notes the ratio of national staff to international staff and the proposed nationalization of six posts and positions and encourages the Mission in its efforts to nationalize functions commensurate with mission mandates and requirements, wherever appropriate.
Vacant posts

35.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 28 February 2026, there were 955 vacant posts and positions (4 D-1, 13 P-5, 51 P-4, 67 P-3, 7 P-2, 56 National Professional Officer, 122 Field Service, 463 national General Service and 170 international United Nations Volunteer and 2 national United Nations Volunteer). As at 31 December 2025, 12 posts (1 P-3, 1 P-2, 1 National Professional Officer and 9 national General Service) had been vacant for two years or longer. The Advisory Committee notes the high number of vacant posts and trusts that information on the status of vacant posts will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission and that all long-vacant posts will be critically reviewed in the next budget period.

36.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 31 December 2025, 152 posts were encumbered by staff with approved requests for the early separation programme. The Advisory Committee trusts that detailed and updated information on all staff of the Mission approved for the early separation programme, as well as on related costs, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission.
Vacancy rates
Vacancy rates
(Percentage)

37.	The Advisory Committee notes the difference between the actual and proposed vacancy rates for international staff, national staff and international United Nations Volunteers. In particular, the proposed vacancy rates for international and national staff are significantly lower than the current actual rates, and the vacancy factors take into account the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27 (ibid., para. 85). The Committee was informed that the proposed vacancy rates are also based on the assumption that recruitment restrictions will be lifted, enabling the Mission to maintain mandate delivery without severe operational limitations. The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretary-General will provide updated information on the latest actual and actual average vacancy rates to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, along with clear justification where the proposed rates differ from the actual rates, including as regards the proposed abolishments, realistic projected recruitment and potential upcoming vacancies, to the extent possible.
38.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for civilian personnel.
3.	Operational costs
(Thousands of United States dollars)

39.	The proposed requirements of $290,528,600 reflect a decrease of $22,452,400, or 7.2 per cent, for operational costs, mainly under facilities and infrastructure, air operations, communications and information technology, ground transportation, marine operations and other supplies, services and equipment (ibid., sects. II and III).

40.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, for group III (operational costs), liquidity challenges resulted in the postponement of projects and deferred acquisitions for communications and information technology, infrastructure and operational requirements during the 2025/26 budget period. The proposed budget for 2026/27 was formulated based on the planning assumptions relevant for the period, taking into account the operational necessity and the postponement.
Consultants and consulting services

41.	The proposed resources for consultants and consulting services amount to $884,700 for 2026/27, representing no change compared with the approved resources for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $669,600 and $1,001,100 against the appropriations of $951,900 and $1,331,800 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. Taking into account the pattern of underexpenditure, the Advisory Committee recommends a reduction of 10 per cent ($39,200) to the proposed resources for consultants and consulting services, excluding resources in support of electoral activities (see para. 24 above).
Official travel

42.	The proposed resources for official travel amount to $3,407,300, reflecting a decrease of $42,400, or 1.2 per cent, compared with the approved level of $3,449,700 for 2025/26. The Secretary-General indicates that, in 2024/25, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the lower actual number of trips compared with the budgeted estimates owing to austerity measures established to manage the ongoing liquidity challenges (A/80/621, para. 115). The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $2,667,800 and $2,213,900 against appropriations of $3,895,600 and of $2,468,100 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. The Advisory Committee emphasizes that greater use of on-the-job training, virtual meetings, online platforms and online training tools should be made, and reiterates its view that travel for conferences, workshops and professional meetings should be kept to a minimum in order to achieve efficiencies. In view of the level of expenditure in recent years, and taking into consideration the reduced number of staff in the Mission, the Committee recommends a reduction of 10 per cent ($314,200) to the proposed resources for official travel, excluding resources in support of electoral activities (see also para. 24 above).
Ground transportation

43.	The proposed resources for ground transportation amount to $12,446,900 for 2026/27, a decrease of $902,400, or 6.8 per cent, compared with the approved level of $13,349,300 for 2025/26, attributable primarily to the lower volume and cost of fuel compared with the approved budget and to fewer vehicles proposed for replacement in 2026/27 than originally planned (A/80/621, para. 120). The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $12,274,400 and $13,557,500 against appropriations of $15,129,600 and of $14,469,800 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. The Committee also notes a projected fleet of 1,727 vehicles as at 30 June 2026 and the proposed write-off of 251 vehicles by 30 June 2026. Taking into account the past expenditure pattern, the Advisory Committee recommends a reduction of 5 per cent ($221,400) to the proposed resources for the acquisition of vehicles under ground transportation. The Committee trusts that, in addressing its vehicle replacement requirements in the context of ongoing liquidity constraints, the Mission will continue to explore opportunities to utilize available vehicles from other missions, as appropriate.
Petrol, oil and lubricants

44.	A total provision of $47,595,400 is proposed for fuel, oil and lubricants for the 2026/27 period, a decrease of $13,556,300, or 28.5 per cent, due mainly to changes in fuel costs and lower estimated required litres of fuel in the budget period. From the information provided to it, the Advisory Committee notes the efficiency measures of the Mission and the reduced volume of fuel proposed under facilities and infrastructure, ground transportation, marine operations and air operations. The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on fuel costs will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report. The Committee discusses fuel costs and management in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Quick-impact projects

45.	Information on quick-impact projects is provided in paragraphs 90 and 91 of the report of the Secretary-General, with proposed resources of $4,000,000, reflecting no change compared with 2025/26, for 80 projects. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, for the 2024/25 period, 62 projects were successfully implemented across the 10 states, helping to provide critical infrastructure to strengthen the justice chain, to bridge the service delivery gap in health and education, and to support the provision of potable drinking water to conflict-affected populations. In the 2025/26 period, 80 projects were initially planned; however, due to the implementation of the 15 per cent cost reduction, that number was reduced to 69. In the 2026/27 period, the Mission plans to increase the number of projects to 80, as they continue to be a major source of infrastructure funding for critical services and the strengthening of the justice chain.

46.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, in the context of the implementation of the contingency plan, the Mission is restructuring its approach to implementing the projects and is applying a remote-monitoring mechanism, as outlined in the Mission’s standard operating procedures (2024) for quick-impact projects. Furthermore, the Mission is not directly involved in project implementation, as its standard operating procedures emphasize that project implementation should be supported and performed by national, international and local non-governmental organizations, community-based organizations and United Nations agencies, funds and programmes as implementing partners. The Committee was informed that, following the closure of the four field locations, communities and local authorities have expressed concern about whether the Mission will continue to implement projects, as the Mission remains their only reliable source for addressing urgent service delivery gaps and strengthening local institutional capacity. An expansion of projects has also been requested due to the multiple challenges faced by communities in conflict-affected areas. The Advisory Committee trusts that UNMISS will ensure that projects benefit the local population, as intended, and that updated information, including on planning, implementation and assessment, will be provided in the context of the next proposed budget report.
47.	Subject to the recommendations contained in paragraphs 24, 41, 42 and 43 above, the Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for operational costs.
Environmental initiatives

48.	The report of the Secretary-General indicates that UNMISS will continue to reduce its environmental footprint through the construction of concrete septic tanks, a conventional sludge wastewater treatment plant in Bor and ammunition storage areas to address environmental and safety concerns. Efficiencies resulting from the integration of the contingent-owned power generation system into the United Nations-owned power grid and the connection of Tomping camp in Juba and Bor camp to the local grid power supply are also indicated (A/80/621, paras. 47 and 78). The Advisory Committee notes the efforts of UNMISS to improve its environmental footprint and trusts that further efficiency gains will be generated from energy and environmental initiatives and will be reflected in future reports.
Situation at Tomping logistics base

49.	The Advisory Committee recalls the matter of the Tomping logistics base and the eviction notice issued by the host Government (A/79/724/Add.12, para. 48). Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that relocation was assessed as not feasible due to the extremely short time frame, substantial financial implications, significant operational disruption and concerns regarding encroachment at potential alternative sites. Ultimately, the host Government agreed that UNMISS would remain at Tomping for the time being. The Committee was informed that, in the spirit of cooperation with the Government and recognizing the mutual benefits of enhanced aviation safety, UNMISS supported the South Sudan civil aviation authority in its efforts to upgrade safety standards at Juba International Airport, and an area of approximately 60 km² adjacent to the taxiway was identified for handover. The Committee was further informed that Tomping remains an ongoing matter on the Government’s agenda and that, while the Mission is open to discussions on its future, any relocation is required to be cost neutral and to not compromise the Mission’s ability to implement its mandate. The Advisory Committee trusts that any relevant updates will be provided to the Assembly in a timely manner.
Sexual exploitation and abuse

50.	The Advisory Committee was provided with information regarding sexual exploitation and abuse cases in UNMISS. To date in 2026, no cases have been reported. Of the five pending cases, three are pending investigation by the Office of Internal Oversight Services or by the police-contributing country in which the implicated personnel are United Nations police officers; and one is in the process of referral for criminal accountability, and the implicated staff member was separated on agreed termination on 12 February 2026. In the final case, the staff member has been separated, and the process of referral for criminal accountability will be initiated. The Committee was also informed that the Mission has a robust outreach programme in place that raises awareness of sexual exploitation and abuse among local communities through direct engagement and radio public service announcements. UNMISS also has workplans for all field locations in which locally recruited staff are reminded of their obligations as international civil servants to avoid harmful cultural practices and respect local laws, and to comply with international standards. The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretary-General will pursue further efforts to prevent sexual exploitation and abuse, ensure the accountability of perpetrators and provide protection and support to the victims, and that information thereon will be provided in future reports (see also resolution 76/274, sect. IV, and resolution 78/331). The Committee further discusses protection from sexual exploitation and abuse in the context of its report on special measures for protection from sexual exploitation and abuse (A/80/691).
Management of sea containers

51.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 28 February 2026, UNMISS held 6,899 sea containers, of which 6,743 are in use, 155 are in transit and 1 is in stock, as follows: (a) 4,190 warehousing containers, which are stationary and used primarily for storage and supply support; (b) 2,553 shipping containers, used for cargo movement across the mission area and along international supply corridors; (c) containers used for temporary shelter, offices, workshops and accommodation units and primarily drawn from the warehousing category; and (d) written-off and repurposed containers used for security enhancement and engineering use, where operationally required. The Committee discusses the management of sea containers in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Equitable geographical representation and gender parity

52.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 31 December 2025, UNMISS comprised 2,152 staff at all levels. Among the 852 international staff, 115 nationalities were represented. Of the total 2,152 staff, 494 were female and 1,658 were male.

53.	As at 31 December 2025, international staff were from the following regional groups: African States (445); Asia-Pacific States (174); Eastern European States (97); Latin American and Caribbean States (27); and Western European and other States (79), as well as from the United States of America (25) and others (5).

54.	The Advisory Committee trusts that UNMISS will further increase its efforts to promote equitable geographical representation and gender parity. The Committee further discusses equitable geographical representation and gender parity in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

55.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of UNMISS for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 are indicated in section IV of the report of the Secretary-General (A/80/621). The Advisory Committee notes the unencumbered balance of $38,504,900 for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025, as well as other revenue amounting to $12,319,800 from investment revenue ($5,510,400), other/miscellaneous revenue ($195,400) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($6,614,000) for the period ended 30 June 2025. The Committee also notes the proposal of the Secretary-General to place the credits arising from the above-mentioned revenue and adjustments in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates. The Committee considers this is a policy matter to be decided by the General Assembly. The Committee makes further comments in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

56.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of UNMISS for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 are indicated in section IV of the report of the Secretary-General (A/80/621). Taking into account its observations and recommendations above, the Advisory Committee recommends that the proposed resources be reduced by $773,000, from $1,057,591,600 to $1,056,818,600. Accordingly, the Committee recommends that the General Assembly appropriate the amount of $1,056,818,600 for the maintenance of the Mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027. A summary of the budgetary impact and financial implications resulting from the recommendations of the Committee with regard to the financing of UNMISS for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 is presented in table 4.

Table 4 – Budgetary impact and financial implications resulting from the recommendations of the Advisory Committee for the United Nations Mission in South Sudan for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027

table_content.text: Reduction of 5 per cent (acquisition of vehicles)
